title: Laying vinyl sheet flooring
subtitle: Cut and fit a single room using the scribe-and-trim method
excerpt: How to lay a full-width roll of vinyl sheet flooring in a domestic room: preparing the subfloor, templating the floor plan with paper, cutting the vinyl sheet to fit, and securing the edges with adhesive or trim.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. City and Guilds Level 2 Flooring syllabus.

content.text: Vinyl sheet flooring is one of the more tolerant floor coverings to lay. It requires no adhesive in most domestic rooms (loose lay with perimeter trim is the domestic standard), it can be cut with a knife, and it handles uneven floors better than timber or ceramic. The critical step is preparation of the
subfloor
: vinyl is thin and reveals every bump beneath it.
What you need
Vinyl sheet flooring (roll). Measure the room, add 100 mm on each dimension for waste. If the room is wider than the roll, budget for a seam.
Threshold strip (aluminium or PVC) for the doorway transitions.
Perimeter trim or double-sided tape for the edges (if not using skirting to hold the vinyl).
Scribing block (a 25 mm timber offcut works) and pencil.
Self-levelling compound for hollows in the subfloor deeper than 3 mm.
Step 1, prepare the subfloor

Remove all door strips and undercut the door linings if the vinyl adds enough height to stop doors swinging. Punch all nail and screw heads in a timber subfloor below the surface; a raised head under vinyl becomes a visible and growing lump. Sand down any high points. Fill dips and hollows deeper than 3 mm with a floor-levelling compound, allow to cure, and sand smooth. The subfloor must be clean, dry, and free of grit before the vinyl goes down.
Step 2, cut the vinyl sheet roughly to size

Unroll the vinyl face-down on a clean flat surface (another room, a garage). Measure the room dimensions, add 100 mm on all sides, and cut roughly to this size. Roll the vinyl loosely (face outward) to transport it to the room and unroll it face-up, laying it so it laps up the walls by about 50 mm all around.
Step 3, scribe to fit

Push the vinyl into one corner so it is in the correct position across the room. Using a scribing block (a 25 mm offcut of timber) and a pencil, draw the scribing block along the wall while marking a line on the vinyl 25 mm from the wall profile. The line on the vinyl mirrors the wall outline, including any bumps and irregularities. Cut along this line with a sharp utility knife and a straight-edge where the wall is straight. The
scribing
removes the excess 25 mm from all edges while fitting the vinyl precisely to the wall profile. Repeat for each wall.
Step 4, cut around obstacles

For a toilet pedestal or pipe emerging from the floor, use a template made from newspaper or lining paper. Cut the template to fit tightly around the obstacle, transfer it to the vinyl, and cut it out. For a simple round pipe, fold the vinyl to the centre of the pipe, cut a small notch, and open it into a hole. It is better to cut a notch slightly too small and enlarge it than to cut too much at once.
Step 5, secure the perimeter

In domestic rooms, a loose-lay vinyl held by reinstated skirting or threshold strips is the standard. Where the vinyl must be fully bonded (in bathrooms, kitchens, or where the floor receives heavy wear), apply a thin bed of vinyl flooring adhesive to the subfloor around the perimeter (300 mm in from each wall), press the vinyl down, and roll it with a floor roller or tamp it firmly with a rubber mallet block. Butt the threshold strip into the doorway and screw or stick it down.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Lift the vinyl from the nearest edge, locate the bump in the subfloor, and remove it. Punch the nail below the surface and refill, or sand down the high point. Re-lay the vinyl.
attrs.cause: A nail head, screw, or piece of grit in the subfloor was not removed before laying.
attrs.symptom: Bumps or ridges appear through the vinyl after laying

attrs.fix: Use the same scribing block for all walls. Keep the block pressed flat against the wall and the pencil vertical throughout the stroke.
attrs.cause: The scribing block was not held flat against the wall throughout, or the block size was different on different walls.
attrs.symptom: Gaps at the wall after scribing

attrs.fix: Lift the edge, clean both surfaces, allow to dry, and rebond with a waterproof vinyl flooring adhesive. Seal the perimeter edge with a thin bead of sanitary silicone.
attrs.cause: Edges not bonded with adhesive in a wet area; water ingress under the vinyl is softening the adhesive layer or the subfloor.
attrs.symptom: Vinyl edges lifting in a bathroom after a few months
